PASSAGE:
You take her hands in yours, feeling the tremor in her fingers as the morning light filters through the ivy-covered walls. "Sarah, I need you to understand something first. Three years ago, when I was assigned to this planet, to this coffee shop, to you—it was just a mission. But somewhere between your terrible jokes and the way you hum while you work, I stopped being what I was supposed to be." Your voice cracks as alien extraction teams fan out across Fremont just blocks away, their inhuman movements coordinated through frequencies your severed communication device can no longer receive. "When I attacked Kira back there, when I chose to protect you instead of completing my mission, I didn't just betray my handlers. I betrayed my entire species. Every alien on that ship, every scout like me—they're all going to die because I fell in love with a human." Sarah's photographer eyes search your face, and you can see her processing the impossible. "The harvest was supposed to begin today. Millions of humans, including you, were going to disappear. But I couldn't do it, Sarah. I couldn't let them take you, even if it means my people will execute me for treason." The distant sound of coordinated footsteps echoes from the street as the extraction teams close their perimeter, but Sarah's grip on your hands tightens instead of pulling away.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Explain how the alien technology works and what the harvest truly means for humanity
B: Focus on the immediate danger—the extraction teams are closing in and you need to move
C: Ask Sarah what she wants to do now that she knows everything
D: Show her the photographic evidence she captured that could expose the entire invasion

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

data.passagePreview: You reach into your jacket and pull out Sarah's camera, the one she dropped during the chase. Your h

data.passage:
You reach into your jacket and pull out Sarah's camera, the one she dropped during the chase. Your hands shake as you turn it toward her, scrolling through the digital display. "You already have the proof," you whisper. "You've been documenting everything without knowing it."

Sarah's eyes widen as image after image flickers past—surveillance operatives moving with inhuman coordination, the geometric patterns of alien communication devices she mistook for street art, crowds of people whose shadows fall at impossible angles. Her photographer's eye had captured what her conscious mind couldn't process.

"Oh my God," she breathes, gripping the camera. "How many are there?"

"Thousands. Maybe more. They've been here for months, preparing." You watch her face transform as the magnitude hits her. "Sarah, that camera contains evidence that could save humanity. But if you publish those photos, if you expose us..."

You can't finish the sentence. You both know what it means—your people will accelerate the harvest, eliminate witnesses, disappear into the cosmos before humanity can mount a defense. Billions will die in the panic that follows.

"And if I don't?" Her voice is steady now, the terror replaced by something harder.

"They complete the harvest slowly, carefully. Taking people gradually so no one notices until it's too late." You meet her eyes. "Either way, your species dies. The only question is whether you fight or sleep through it."

Sarah stands, camera clutched to her chest like armor. Above you, the first alien ships begin descending through the morning clouds, no longer hiding. The invasion has moved beyond subtlety.

"Joe," she says, and there's love in her voice alongside the resolve. "Thank you for choosing us."

She kisses you once, soft and final, then runs toward the street. Toward the newspaper offices. Toward humanity's last desperate chance.

You don't follow. Love, it turns out, sometimes means letting go.
